Client 1: Yep.

Client 1: We do have rental income—8900 a year, paid monthly.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. Now on the spending side—Linda, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely. Total monthly expenses are 4350 a month.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. When you say that, is that including any debt payments, like a mortgage, car loan, credit cards—anything like that?
Client 2: No, we don’t have those anymore.

Client 1: Yes, US.
Advisor: Great. Now, risk—when markets get choppy, what’s your comfort level? I know you said you like it simple, Linda.
Client 2: Very cautious. I’d say conservative.
Advisor: Okay—conservative. That helps me frame how much volatility we should even consider.
Advisor: When you say conservative, is that more like “don’t lose money,” or more like “some ups and downs are okay as long as it’s not scary”?

Advisor: Hi James, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. I’m 62, still working, and I’m trying to make sure we’re actually set up to retire without doing anything reckless.
Client 2: And I’m Linda—71. I’m already retired, so I mostly want it to feel steady month to month.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it practical. I’m going to start with the big picture cash flow—income coming in, then spending, then what you’ve got saved. Sound okay?

Advisor: Hi James, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. I’m 62, still working, and I’m trying to make sure we’re actually set up to retire without doing anything reckless.
Client 2: And I’m Linda—71. I’m already retired, so I mostly want it to feel steady month to month.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it practical. I’m going to start with the big picture cash flow—income coming in, then spending, then what you’ve got saved. Sound okay?

Advisor: Okay. Next one’s simple: what’s your marital status—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Great. And just so I’m clear on how you want to work together with me—are you engaging me through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. Thanks. Last household item: when you think about your federal bracket, where do you usually land—roughly low twenties, mid twenties, higher?

Advisor: NY—got it. And you file from there, same state residency year-round?
Client 2: Yes, NY year-round.
Advisor: Okay. Next one’s simple: what’s your marital status—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Great. And just so I’m clear on how you want to work together with me—are you engaging me through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, versus like a brokerage-only setup?

Client 2: I always mix that up—James handles it.
Advisor: Totally fine. James, what bracket band do you typically plan around?
Client 1: 24%.
Advisor: Got it—24%. One sec, I’m just jotting those down.
Advisor: And just to connect it back to what we covered: you’ve got the three income streams—Linda’s pension, James’s salary, and the rental income—then the brokerage account, the retirement account, the bank balance on the advisor platform, that private investment, and the home; no loans outstanding and no protection policies in force right now. We’ll use the NY and 24% assumptions when we run the projections next time.

Client 1: Yes, employed.
Advisor: Okay. Now income—just your gross annual income from work, before taxes and deductions. What number should I use for you, James?
Client 1: I think it’s around sixty-ish. Hang on—gross annual is 61050.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And Linda, you’re retired, and most of your income is that pension we talked about, correct?
Client 2: Yes, I’m retired—pension is the big one for me.

Advisor: Okay. James, I also see something that sounds like a private investment held through an insurance company—does that ring a bell?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s right. It’s 36250.

Client 2: We have it at 166350.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And just to confirm on the debt side—you said no loans outstanding at all right now, correct?

Client 2: It’s basically the pension. I think it’s like forty-something.
Advisor: Totally fine—ballpark helps. If you had to give me the exact annual gross number you want me to use, what is it?

Advisor: Perfect—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing terms: that 44500 is the annual gross amount, not monthly.
Client 2: Right—annual. Monthly would be way too high.
